result:
The first thing that comes to me is the sound of the refrigerator. A low, planetary hum I haven’t truly *heard* in years, not since I first moved into a place of my own and lay awake, amazed by the silence that was never silent. It’s there now, beneath the tap-tap of these keys. A baseline. The sound of preservation, of held chill, of things kept in stasis against the entropy of the warm room.

It makes me think of cold, and then of a specific cold: the iron railing of the balcony on my grandmother’s apartment, winter of 1994. I must have been seven or eight. I licked it, on a dare from myself. The instant, shocking bond between warm, wet skin and frozen metal. A pain so pure and bright it felt like a revelation. I tore my tongue away, leaving, I was sure, a layer of myself behind. I tasted blood and snow. For a week, every sip of soup was a reminder of that impulsive act. A lesson in consequence. But here’s the strange part: I remember it fondly. The memory holds no trauma, only the vivid, electric aliveness of the moment. The sheer, stupid poetry of being a creature in a physical world.

We spend so much time trying to avoid that railing-lick. To be comfortable, safe, preserved in the hum of our own making. We pad the corners, thermostat the climate, filter the water, curate the intake. And yet, the moments we recall, the ones that pulse with color decades later, are almost always the breaches. The unexpected conversations, the missed trains that led to strange adventures, the heartbreaks that cracked us open, the public humiliations that later became comic fodder, the sudden, unbidden beauty of a shaft of light on a dusty floor. The times the insulation failed.

The writer Annie Dillard said we spend our lives "saving our lives." We board up the windows against the storm. But the storm, she implies, is where the life is. Not in the cataclysm itself, but in the raw, unmediated engagement with the weather of being. The lick of the railing.

From my window, I can see a maple tree. It’s mid-season, caught between the lush, confident green of summer and the fiery surrender of autumn. A few leaves have already turned—precocious, or perhaps just tired. They cling, these orange flames, to a still-green branch. The tree doesn’t seem to mind its own inconsistency. It doesn’t strive for a unified aesthetic. It just is, moment by moment, cell by cell, responding to the diminishing light, the cooler nights. It is engaged in the profound, slow-motion drama of letting go. It doesn’t *save* its life; it spends it, gloriously, in a cascade of chlorophyll breakdown and carotenoid revelation.

We are so afraid of spending. We are taught to hoard: money, time, affection, energy. We are taught that security lies in the stockpile. But what is a stocked life? A refrigerator full of untouched, perfectly preserved meals, their sell-by dates ticking down in the dark? The hum becomes a dirge.

Maybe the real art is in the wise spending. Knowing when to lick the railing. Knowing that some pain is not a warning, but a credential of participation. The crack in the vase where the light gets in, as Leonard Cohen, that great poet of the fissure, assured us. We are here to be permeable. To let the world in, even when it’s cold and it sticks. To let ourselves out, even when it’s risky.

I think of all the love I’ve tried to preserve in the chill of caution, of not wanting to appear foolish, of wanting to keep things *just so*. How much more life there was in the loves I spent recklessly, generously, with no guarantee of return. The friendships forged in shared, unvarnished truth. The ideas blurted out before they were fully formed. These were the expenditures that accrued interest in the currency of memory, of a life felt rather than managed.

The refrigerator clicks off. The hum ceases, and for a few seconds, the silence is absolute, a pool of quiet so deep I can hear the faint tinnitus ring in my own ears—the sound of my living system. Then it kicks on again, that faithful, mechanical cycle.

But I am not a refrigerator. My purpose is not to maintain a constant, safe temperature. My purpose is to feel the weather. To know the scorch of the pavement and the bite of the rail. To turn, like the maple, not all at once in a showy, synchronized performance, but leaf by leaf, in my own time, as the internal light dictates.

It will happen. One day, the system will stop its cycling for good. The hum will be final. And on that day, I hope the ledger of my life doesn’t show a cautious balance, safely preserved. I hope it shows a wild and generous deficit. I hope it shows I spent every last bit of warmth I had, that I licked every railing that intrigued me, that I left nothing in the freezer for a tomorrow that never came. That I felt the blood and the snow, and knew it for what it was: the fierce, fleeting, delicious taste of being here at all.

The world is not a pantry. It is a feast, and it is happening right now, in the messy, warm, perilous, beautiful room outside the hum. The door is right there. Unseal it. Let the light flood the chilled, ordered shelves. Let the moths in. Let the butter soften. Step out. Lick something.

Word count: 799
